design_philosophy.core_loop: Allocate resources across military, economy, and peasant loyalty to expand territory and influence, then resolve conflicts through tactical hex-based battles
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing aggressive expansion (military investment, taxation) against peasant loyalty and rebellion risk; managing succession as daimyos age and die
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Unify Japan as a historical daimyo through political acumen, military strategy, and economic management during the Sengoku period
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Treats strategy gaming as a complex economic/diplomatic/political simulation rather than purely tactical warfare; emphasizes systemic consequences (taxation causing rebellion, cultivation decreasing loyalty) over combat spectacle

difficulty.checkpointing: Turn-based progression with seasonal/yearly time system allows gradual adjustment of strategy
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High degree of player choice in resource allocation, diplomacy, and campaign tempo; players can pass turns to train troops or reassess strategy; multiple victory conditions offer flexible pathways

progression.upgrade_system: Military unit training increases fighting efficiency; infrastructure development (flood control, town expansion) increases productivity; recruitment of soldiers and ninja; weapon purchases
progression.unlock_mechanism: Four campaign scenarios with different starting years (1560–1582) and initial conditions; 50-province map expands game scope; daimyo succession system creates dynamic progression as leaders age
replayability_hooks: Multiple selectable daimyos with different starting positions
Four distinct campaign scenarios with varied initial conditions
Dynamic succession system where daimyos age and die of old age
Multiple victory conditions (enemy retreat, command unit destruction, supply exhaustion, prolonged survival)

world_building_techniques: Historical Sengoku period setting with real daimyos and provinces
Time system anchors narrative progression (each turn = one year; daimyos age and die naturally)
Economic and political systems reflect historical tensions (peasant rebellion, marriages/pacts as diplomatic tools)
Hex map with 50 provinces grounds player in geographic/territorial context

faction_indicators: Choice of daimyo defines faction and historical allegiance
Non-aggression pacts and marriages establish diplomatic relationships
Rival daimyos compete for province control and peasant loyalty
danger_signals: Peasant rebellion risk from over-taxation and over-cultivation
Military recruitment and training for impending war
environmental_storytelling: Province-level fief management shows economic health and loyalty
Flood control and town expansion indicate civilization development
Military unit allocation between fiefs reflects defensive or offensive posture
Seasonal map view progression represents historical timeline progression
